chapters.title: Without a strict healthy eating habit, food addiction can take hold quickly
chapters.text:
Many adults struggle with food addiction and, as a result, become obese. This is unfortunate and unhealthy for anyone. Obesity comes from overeating and a sedentary life. Once you lose control of your eating, the next stop on the track is addiction.

Cultivating healthy eating habits is important to avoid illness and other complications.

Most people think losing weight should be easy. They were not always obese, so surely they can get back there. The sad truth is that climbing out of food addiction is hard. Like any addiction, food addiction pushes you to keep eating long after you are full, trying to fill a craving. Even if you just ate, you eat again until you cannot move.

Addiction is dangerous. Discipline is what protects us from overindulging in our worst habits.

Here is where things get tricky. Your mind starts playing games with you. It insists there is no way out and tells you to accept your fate. You must not give in. Your health matters more than the comfort of old habits, and you have to take it back. The market is full of quick fixes and self-help guides that promise to cure food addiction and obesity. Most of them do not help. They only frustrate and discourage people who are desperate for change, pushing them into another downward spiral. This summary is different. The principles and tactics here come from real experiments tested on thousands of people.

Quick-fix solutions are almost always false. Look for advice from trained professionals.

In the chapters ahead, we will cover everyday harmful eating habits, how to fight them, and how to return to peak fitness. Do not commit to any quick fix. They are usually designed to take your money. Glenn Livingstone, PhD, is no ordinary hack. He is a trained professional whose methods are backed by extensive research.

Recovery begins with honesty about the trap you are in.

The story usually starts the same way. A bad day at work, a fight with a friend, or a slow wave of boredom, and suddenly the fridge feels like the only place that makes sense. The binge feels good for a few minutes. Then the guilt arrives, and you promise yourself tomorrow will be different. Tomorrow comes, and the same pull returns. That cycle is the trap, and breaking it takes more than willpower. It takes a clear plan and a way to talk back to the voice in your head.

What follows in this book is that plan.

chapters.title: Building a food plan that stops the binge cycle
chapters.text:
Bingeing on food is one of the toughest addictions to break. Unlike drugs or alcohol, you cannot just stop eating. You have to decide what to eat, where to eat, and how to eat. That decision drags people onto a ride very few can step off. Many find diets and food plans hard to follow. The standard formulas available today are too rigid to fit the many different faces of bingeing.

Food addiction comes from an inability to control how much we eat.

One reason we cannot stick to a diet is the inner pig. The pig stands for the part of you that thinks only about food. It hijacks your survival drive and pushes you toward eating habits that do NOT serve your best interests. It is an out-of-control eating machine that will ruin everything if you let it. It sabotages your plans and exists only to feed itself.

You have power over your mind, not over outside events. Realize that, and you will find strength, as Marcus Aurelius once wrote.

The pig is not you, and you have everything it takes to control it. You can cage it and follow the food plan that works for you. For this to succeed, you have to take one hundred percent responsibility for setting up your plan. It is fine to ask a nutritionist for advice, but you have to choose what works.

Your food plan should be crystal clear. Write it down and pin it to a wall so you can see when you are slipping. As you start, listen for the squeals of the pig, the voice that tries to block your progress. To quiet it, set up rules that guide you through the process. First, define the eating habits, or habits in general, that you will never indulge in again. For example, pick a food or food group to drop for good. Second, define the habits you will always follow, like eating more vegetables, drinking more water, or working out. Third, choose eating habits that your plan does not restrict, and label those foods as unrestricted. Fourth, place some foods under conditional, and define the exceptions that allow them. For example, you might decide to drink soda only after finishing a workout.

chapters.title: Discomfort is normal as you follow your new food plan
chapters.text:
Some discomfort is normal as you walk this road. You are at war with the pig inside you, and it will remind you of every pleasure you got from junk food. It will tell you that giving up a food group is slowly killing you. It will say that you are suffering and that you are suffering a lot.

In truth, discomfort is a normal response and should not be treated as a reason to quit.

You will feel like part of you is slipping away. You will face your cravings head on. And in a society that celebrates unhealthy eating, you will feel overwhelmed by your own choices. You might agree that there is no rest for those who decide to eat right.

Still, there is a way to win no matter how much pressure builds against you. You should never forget your food plan and your Nevers, Always, Unrestricteds, and Conditionals. Remember that every voice telling you to give up is the pig. Keep reminding yourself of the promise you made never to binge again or never to indulge in unhealthy food.

If you slip and binge, you have to know how to get back on track, or you will give up entirely. To stay ahead of your fears, guilt, regrets, and failures, you must rely on progress rather than perfection. There are many reasons not to chase perfection. One of them is that the pig can use your perfectionism against you when you fall. It will tell you that you are weak and broken, that you cannot do right by yourself, that you cannot keep your word. So you must build a framework that defends your confidence and your will to keep going.

To pull yourself back up, identify what caused the binge. Then review your food plan and figure out which of your Conditionals is the root cause. Move that Conditional into the Never category. Most importantly, understand that one slip should not stain your commitment to the Never Binge Again practice.

chapters.title: Knowing when to change your food plan and when to leave it alone
chapters.text:
Change is part of life, and your food plan will need to change too. New research might reveal flaws in your current plan. Stay open to those changes. Just do not let your instinct to improve become an excuse to feed the pig.

Identify your main reason for change, the new rules you want to introduce, and why the change matters.

Glenn Livingstone, PhD, has said he does not believe in the numbering system some addicts use to mark their progress. For example, some people count the days since they last gave in. That approach keeps them stuck in a battle against their impulses, reminding them that they cannot live a normal life.

Instead, Livingstone advises stripping the pig of any recognition or power that would let it pop back up when you make mistakes. Alongside that, learn what deprivation is doing for you. What is your abstinence from unhealthy eating giving you? That question will help you stay committed to your plan.

It is hard to stick to a plan when the food industry keeps trying to sell you unhealthy products dressed up as healthy ones. Look past the fancy packaging and see the truth underneath.

Do not wait for other people's approval before doing what you know you should.

Take full ownership of your food plan. Do not look for validation from anyone else. You can also keep this Never Binge Again philosophy to yourself. Many people in similar situations are still trapped in the belief that they need help, so they may push back against you because your success reminds them that breaking free is possible.

chapters.title: You have the power to choose what you eat
chapters.text:
Many people believe that addiction is a disease in itself, and that those who suffer from it can never fully fight it off. That false idea convinces victims that they are powerless and that the best they can do is spend their whole lives nursing this mystery disease.

People get brainwashed into thinking relapses are normal, that full recovery is impossible. That belief cripples the minds of those battling food addiction. They feel powerless and find something to blame when they slip. A mindset built on responsibility does the opposite.

When you take responsibility for where you are and believe you have the power to do better, change becomes easier.

Never let your cravings decide who you are. Take control of them instead.

Along the way, watch out for the common mistakes that send people back to square one. You might be tempted to take a short break from your diet because you are seeing some early progress. You might decide to indulge in a Never food because you can burn it off in your next workout. It is also common for people to slip into an unhealthy lifestyle while telling themselves they are practicing self-love.

No matter how you might stumble, you have to stay focused on the task. Stay true to the commitment and the promise you made to eat right. Remember that the cost of failure is far heavier than the temporary pleasure of junk food. With that in mind, lean on discipline. It is the only path to real freedom.

Long ago, the ancient people of South America used chocolate as a form of currency.

chapters.title: Beware of guilt and shame as you fight the urge to binge
chapters.text:
Guilt and shame act as flags that remind us we have done something wrong. They show up when you start the Never Binge Again program. When you slip, they remind you of the mistake and tell you that you have gone off course.

Emotions are useful signals, but holding on to them too long is dangerous.

Do not feed on guilt and shame by beating yourself up until you start doubting your ability to stay the course. Instead, acknowledge the guilt and shame, learn from the mistake, and plan a fix that will help you stay immune to that temptation in the future. In hindsight, past failures make you stronger, especially when you learn to channel the energy they release.

Anyone who has never made a mistake has never tried anything new, as Albert Einstein once said.

Stay alert mentally. It is common for people to blame unconscious bingeing for their relapses. That idea is false. We tend to ignore what we are doing when it goes against the principles we want to live by. Dr. Livingstone suggests keeping a journal and writing down every way the pig tried to make you fail.

This habit helps you spot those traps in the future and trains your body to become resistant to them. Still, even after you have done all the right things, what happens if you are still struggling?

At that point, you have to rely on your free will to choose healthy food again and again until it sticks. Free will is the force that pushes us to choose our own path. It reminds us that every choice has consequences. So when you reach a crossroads, hold on to your right to choose health, and use it until your addiction fades.

chapters.title: Final thoughts on a healthier life
chapters.text:
More people than we like to admit struggle with food addiction. That addiction leads to serious health problems and, in some cases, death. Food addiction does not appear overnight. It usually grows out of the difficulty of managing how much we eat.

Fighting food addiction takes real willpower and support from people who love you. Once you start on the path to freedom from binging, it is important to take slow, careful steps so your body does not snap back into old habits. A healthy support system is a powerful tool for learning and keeping a new habit.

No matter how ready you think you are, expect outside forces to test your commitment. Your success depends on your ability to tame your mind, learn from mistakes, and keep improving. The goal is to eat better and live healthier. That goal has to be etched into your mind so it becomes the engine that keeps you going when you feel like quitting.

Focus on making progress, however small. There is no rush, as long as you stay committed and steady. If you feel like giving up, consider taking a short break, just long enough to rest but not long enough to slip back into old habits.

Try this

Build a food plan that will give you the body you want. As you build it, remove junk food permanently from your diet and add meals that are both nutritious and low in carbs. Introduce your body to this new way of living in slow, careful steps at a pace that feels comfortable.

Read it till the end?
